editorialNotes: Every connection must have >=1 source with tier="primary" (gov.uk/parliament.uk/court record). Connections with liveProceedings=true render a cautionary banner and must quote only official findings in the public record. Person IDs are kebab-case (surname-given). Counterparty IDs match the canonical IDs used elsewhere in Gracchus; adjacent_firm entries carry no ID.

connectionTypes.post_office_appointment: Ex-minister or senior civil servant joined a firm after leaving office
connectionTypes.dual_role: Held private-sector role while still in public office
connectionTypes.paid_consultancy_during_office: Paid by a firm while serving as MP, minister or civil servant
connectionTypes.family_employment: Close family member employed by the firm
connectionTypes.family_ownership: Close family member holds ownership/substantial shareholding in the firm
connectionTypes.spousal_shareholding: Spouse holds a declared shareholding in the firm
connectionTypes.donation_then_contract: Donor (or donor's firm) received a government contract
connectionTypes.vip_lane_referral: Individual referred a firm into a high-priority procurement lane
connectionTypes.shareholding: Politician holds declared shares in the firm
connectionTypes.appg_paid_chair: MP chairs a paid All-Party Parliamentary Group
connectionTypes.personal_connection_then_contract: Contract awarded to a firm linked to a minister's personal connection (neighbour, friend, associate) without competitive tender
connectionTypes.outside_earnings_during_office: Substantial outside income earned by an MP during their time in Parliament, declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests
connectionTypes.other: Connection of interest not fitting the above categories

statuses.closed_with_finding: Regulator or court found against the individual/firm
statuses.live_proceedings: Legal, regulatory or inquiry proceedings are ongoing
statuses.public_record: Documented in official records (Register of Interests, ACOBA log) with no regulatory review
statuses.reported: Surfaced in investigative journalism; no official process

connections.summary: Owen Paterson was paid approximately £100,000 a year as a consultant to Randox Laboratories while sitting as an MP, and was found by the Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards to have committed an 'egregious' breach of paid-advocacy rules by lobbying for the company inside government.
connections.detail:
Paterson took up a paid consultancy with Randox Laboratories in August 2015, reported at c. £100,000 per year, alongside a separate paid role with Lynn's Country Foods. During his period of engagement Randox received more than £500m of UK government COVID testing contracts, including a £133m contract in March 2020 for testing kits and lab processing and a £346m extension awarded later that year.

In October 2021 the Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards and the Commons Committee on Standards concluded that Paterson had 'repeatedly used his privileged position to benefit two companies for whom he was a paid consultant' and had committed 'an egregious case of paid advocacy'. The Committee recommended a 30-day suspension. The Government's attempt to replace the standards process with a new committee (the Leadsom amendment) passed a whipped Commons vote on 3 November 2021 and was abandoned within 24 hours after cross-party opposition. Paterson resigned as MP for North Shropshire on 5 November 2021.

DHSC later released WhatsApp and email exchanges between Paterson and the then-Health Secretary Matt Hancock regarding Randox contracts, disclosed under FOI and reported by Byline Times in February 2022. No criminal proceedings followed; the matter is treated as closed at the regulatory level on the Commissioner's finding.

regulatoryFindings.body: Parliamentary Commissioner for Standards / Committee on Standards
regulatoryFindings.quotedText: No previous case of paid advocacy has seen so many breaches or such a clear pattern of behaviour in failing to separate private and public interests. Mr Paterson's actions were an egregious case of paid advocacy.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: Committee on Standards recommended a 30-day suspension; Paterson resigned as MP on 5 November 2021.

sources.title: Committee on Standards — Third Report: Mr Owen Paterson
Government Releases Private Messages Between Owen Paterson and Matt Hancock Over Randox Contracts
Owen Paterson Is Not an Anomaly
Investigation into the government's contracts with Randox Laboratories Ltd

connections.summary: Baroness Mone referred PPE Medpro into the government's 'VIP / high-priority' procurement lane, after which the firm won c. £203m of DHSC PPE contracts; the High Court has ordered PPE Medpro to repay c. £122m, and a National Crime Agency criminal investigation into her and her husband Doug Barrowman remains active.
connections.detail:
PPE Medpro Ltd was incorporated in May 2020 and awarded two DHSC contracts totalling a reported £203m for face masks and sterile surgical gowns, entered through the 'VIP / high-priority lane' after a referral by Baroness Mone. For more than two years Mone and Doug Barrowman publicly denied any involvement with the firm; they later confirmed, in a BBC interview broadcast in December 2023, that Barrowman was the ultimate beneficial owner. Reporting indicates Barrowman received more than £60m in profits, with £29m transferred to a trust benefiting Mone and her children.

In 2024 the High Court ruled in favour of DHSC in its civil claim against PPE Medpro, ordering the firm to repay c. £122m for surgical gowns later deemed unsuitable for use. That judgment is a closed finding in the public record.

Separately, the National Crime Agency opened a criminal investigation in May 2021 and executed search warrants at Mone and Barrowman's homes and at PPE Medpro offices in April 2022; as of early 2026 that investigation is ongoing. The UK Covid-19 Inquiry declined a late bid by Mone to participate as a core participant in Module 5 (procurement). Mone took leave of absence from the House of Lords in December 2022. Gracchus reports only findings already entered into the public record.

counterparty.name: PPE Medpro Limited
counterparty.note: Tracked Gracchus supplier inside the PPE cronyism cluster; DHSC is the tracked buyer.

financial.personalIncomeDescription: £29m reported transferred to a trust benefiting Mone and her children
financial.relatedContractsDescription: £203m DHSC contracts; £122m High Court repayment order

regulatoryFindings.body: High Court of England and Wales (civil)
regulatoryFindings.quotedText: PPE Medpro is liable to repay the sum of approximately £122m paid under the gowns contract, the gowns having been found not to conform to the contractual specification.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: Judgment in favour of DHSC; PPE Medpro ordered to repay c. £122m. Appeal rights reserved.

sources.title: Investigation into government procurement during the COVID-19 pandemic
PPE Medpro must repay £122m for breach of contract — judgment summary
sources.publisher: Good Law Project (reporting High Court judgment)

sources.title: Baroness Mone: The PPE contract, a VIP line and the lies to the press

connections.summary: Akshata Murty, wife of Rishi Sunak, holds a reported 0.94% stake (c. 39m shares) in Infosys — a UK government IT supplier that has received at least £47m of public contracts since Sunak became Chancellor in February 2020.
connections.detail:
Infosys was co-founded by N. R. Narayana Murty, father of Akshata Murty. Her shareholding in Infosys has been publicly reported at approximately 39m shares (c. 0.94% of the company, valued at around £610m at time of reporting). Rishi Sunak became Chancellor of the Exchequer in February 2020 and served as Prime Minister from October 2022 to July 2024.

Per reporting by Democracy for Sale, Full Fact and LBC, Infosys has received at least £66m in UK public-sector contracts since 2015, with more than £47m of that awarded after Sunak's appointment as Chancellor. A c.50% year-on-year rise in Infosys's UK public-sector invoices was reported for 2023.

The Independent Adviser on Ministers' Interests ruled that Sunak did not need to declare his wife's Infosys stake on parliamentary registers, and the Cabinet Office has stated that neither Sunak nor his ministers were involved in Infosys contract-award decisions. No regulator, court or parliamentary committee has made any finding of wrongdoing against Sunak in relation to Infosys. A separate 2022 controversy concerning Akshata Murty's non-domiciled tax status ended with her voluntarily giving up non-dom status in April 2022.

Gracchus records this connection as a matter of public fact: the shareholding exists, the contracts exist, the timing overlaps with Sunak's tenure. Gracchus does not assert any causal link between the two.

counterparty.name: Infosys Limited
counterparty.note: Tracked in suppliers-summary.json (NHS workforce solution, £1.2bn framework) but not yet present as a canonical supplier node in money-map.json — carried here as adjacent_firm.

financial.personalIncomeDescription: Akshata Murty's stake reported at c. £610m market value at time of reporting (0.94% of Infosys); no direct personal income to Sunak disclosed in the Register
financial.relatedContractsDescription: £47m+ UK public contracts awarded to Infosys since Feb 2020 (>=£66m since 2015)

sources.title: Register of Members' Financial Interests
Revealed: Sunak wife's family firm in line for millions in new public contracts
Rishi Sunak's wife's firm Infosys received 50% boost in public sector invoices in 2023
Company owned by Sunak's in-laws hasn't been given a multi-billion pound NHS contract

connections.summary: David Cameron took a part-time advisory role at Greensill Capital in 2018, more than two years after leaving Downing Street, and in 2020 lobbied HM Treasury and DHSC ministers and officials on Greensill's behalf; the Commons Treasury Committee concluded he showed 'a significant lack of judgement' but had not broken the rules, because the two-year ACOBA cooling-off period had already expired.
connections.detail:
Cameron resigned as Prime Minister in July 2016 and took up a part-time advisory role with Greensill Capital in August 2018 - more than two years after leaving office. Because more than two years had elapsed between his resignation and the appointment, ACOBA's business-appointment rules did not apply and no ACOBA advice was sought or required. Reporting indicates he held share options in Greensill with a nominal value in the tens of millions of pounds.

During the COVID-19 pandemic in spring 2020 Cameron lobbied HM Treasury, including the then-Chancellor Rishi Sunak, and DHSC for Greensill to be granted access to the Covid Corporate Financing Facility (CCFF). The requests were declined. Text messages, emails and call records were later disclosed under pressure from the Treasury Committee.

The Commons Treasury Committee's July 2021 report Lessons from Greensill Capital concluded that Cameron had not broken lobbying rules as they stood, but that the rules had 'insufficient strength' and that his use of informal lobbying routes showed 'a significant lack of judgement'. The Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee and the Boardman Review reached related findings on the wider governance picture.

Greensill Capital is not among Gracchus's 116 core tracker projects; the lobbying target was HM Treasury's CCFF scheme and DHSC, both tracked departments. Gracchus carries this as an adjacent-firm connection.

counterparty.name: Greensill Capital
counterparty.note: Not a Gracchus-tracked prime supplier. The lobbying targeted HM Treasury (CCFF) and DHSC - both tracked departments. Carried as adjacent_firm.

timeframe.periodLabel: Aug 2018 - Mar 2021 (adviser role until Greensill collapse)

financial.personalIncomeDescription: Share options reported with a nominal value in the tens of millions of pounds; precise realisation value disputed / undisclosed after Greensill's 2021 collapse
financial.relatedContractsDescription: No UK government contracts awarded; 2020 lobbying for access to the Covid Corporate Financing Facility was declined

regulatoryFindings.quotedText: Mr Cameron's engagement with HM Treasury during the COVID-19 pandemic on behalf of Greensill Capital showed a significant lack of judgement, particularly in his use of less formal means to lobby Government.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: Committee concluded Cameron had not broken lobbying rules, but that the rules themselves had 'insufficient strength'.

sources.title: Lessons from Greensill Capital (HC 151)
Propriety of Governance in Light of Greensill
sources.publisher: Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee

sources.title: Cameron's role at Greensill Capital has called the UK's lobbying regulations into question
Cameron exploited lobbying loophole to discuss $1bn China fund with Treasury

connections.summary: Bill Crothers, the Government's Chief Commercial Officer at the Cabinet Office, took up a paid advisory role with Greensill Capital in September 2015 while still a serving civil servant, without declaring the arrangement to the civil service ethics arrangements or seeking ACOBA advice on his post-Crown-service plans; PACAC later found 'too little consideration appears to have been paid to the potential reputational risk'.
connections.detail:
Crothers joined the Cabinet Office as Government Chief Commercial Officer in 2012, with a salary reported up to c. £149,000 per year, and was closely involved in bringing Lex Greensill into government as a 'crown representative'. In September 2015, while still employed in the civil service, Crothers took up a paid advisory role with Greensill Capital. He left the civil service in November 2015 and became a director of Greensill Capital in August 2016.

The Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee's report Propriety of Governance in Light of Greensill found that Crothers had not declared the Greensill role to civil-service ethics arrangements at the time, and had not sought ACOBA's advice on his post-Crown-service role. The Committee concluded that there were 'no obvious conflicts' at the point he was hired by Greensill - because Greensill had limited UK public-sector contracts at that time - but that 'too little consideration appears to have been paid to the potential reputational risk'.

Crothers's Crown Commercial Service role had oversight of the supplier base tracked by Gracchus (Serco, Capita, Atos, Fujitsu, Mitie, G4S and others). The revelation of his dual employment triggered the Boardman Review of civil-service dual employment. Greensill Capital collapsed in March 2021. Gracchus carries this as an adjacent-firm connection.

counterparty.name: Greensill Capital
counterparty.note: Not a Gracchus-tracked prime supplier. Crothers's Crown Commercial Service role nonetheless had oversight of the supplier base tracked by Gracchus. Carried as adjacent_firm.

timeframe.periodLabel: Sep 2015 - Mar 2021 (dual role from Sep-Nov 2015; director to Greensill collapse)

financial.personalIncomeDescription: Civil service salary reported up to c. £149,000 p.a.; Greensill advisory / director remuneration not publicly disclosed
financial.relatedContractsDescription: No direct UK government contracts awarded to Greensill during Crothers's Crown-service period of overlap

regulatoryFindings.body: Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee
regulatoryFindings.quotedText: Too little consideration appears to have been paid to the potential reputational risk of a senior civil servant taking up a paid private-sector role while still in Crown service.
regulatoryFindings.outcome: No breach finding on the point at which Crothers was hired by Greensill, because Greensill had limited UK public-sector contracts at that time; dual-employment arrangement triggered the Boardman Review.

sources.title: Propriety of Governance in Light of Greensill
sources.publisher: Public Administration and Constitutional Affairs Committee

sources.title: ACOBA recommendation - Bill Crothers, Government Chief Commercial Officer, Cabinet Office
Former Whitehall CPO Bill Crothers part of Greensill inquiry

connections.summary: Alex Bourne, the former landlord of Matt Hancock's local pub in the Health Secretary's West Suffolk constituency, secured a contract through his plastics firm Hinpack to supply specimen collection tubes and funnels for NHS COVID-19 testing - a reported ~£30m of DHSC-funded work - after contacting Hancock directly via WhatsApp; Hinpack had no prior PPE or diagnostics manufacturing record.
connections.detail:
Alex Bourne was the landlord of the Cock Inn in Little Thurlow, in Matt Hancock's West Suffolk constituency. In spring 2020 Bourne contacted Hancock via WhatsApp to offer his plastics firm Hinpack's services for the pandemic response. Hinpack had no prior record of manufacturing medical or diagnostic products.

Hinpack went on to partner with a diagnostics supplier to produce specimen collection tubes and funnels for COVID-19 testing, in work that was reported by the Guardian and Good Law Project as amounting to roughly £30m of DHSC-funded contract value. The contract was not awarded through open competitive tender.

Both Hancock and Bourne have publicly stated that Hancock was not personally involved in awarding the contract, and that Hancock told Bourne to apply through the Government's online PPE portal. No regulator, court or parliamentary committee has made a finding of rule-breaking by either individual. Gracchus records this connection because of the public-interest pattern - a direct personal approach to a Secretary of State leading to procurement access - and not because of any proven wrongdoing. DHSC is the tracked buyer; Hinpack is carried as an adjacent firm.

counterparty.name: Hinpack / Alex Bourne
counterparty.note: Matt Hancock's former neighbour, no prior PPE manufacturing record; received ~£30m in DHSC PPE contracts during COVID-19. DHSC (buyer-department-of-health-and-social-care) is the tracked buyer.

financial.personalIncomeDescription: Personal income to Bourne not publicly disclosed
financial.relatedContractsDescription: ~£30m reported DHSC-funded contract value for specimen tubes and funnels

connections.summary: Geoffrey Cox earned more than £6m from outside legal work during his time as Conservative MP for Torridge and West Devon - including more than £800,000 from Withers LLP for advising the Government of the British Virgin Islands in an inquiry set up by the UK Foreign Office to investigate alleged corruption in the BVI - all declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests.
connections.detail:
Cox has practised at the Bar throughout his time as an MP, most prominently through Withers LLP. His outside earnings, declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests, have consistently placed him among the highest-earning MPs of his era. Reporting by ITV News, The Week and Byline Times, drawing on the Register, put his outside earnings since the 2019 general election at more than £2m; wider reporting on his earnings across his time in Parliament places the total above £6m.

His largest single engagement has been advising the Government of the British Virgin Islands in connection with an inquiry set up by the UK Foreign Office to investigate alleged corruption in the BVI. Withers LLP's engagement with the BVI government was reported at a rate of £468,000 per year for 48 hours of work per month, with more than £150,000 specifically for BVI inquiry work and more than £800,000 in aggregate from Withers LLP over the period.

In 2016 the Standards Committee found Cox had committed a 'serious breach' of Commons rules by failing to register c. £400,000 of outside earnings (eleven payments) within the 28-day window. No subsequent breach finding has been made against Cox in relation to the BVI work specifically, and no finding of paid advocacy has been made. Cox maintains that the work does not breach any rules.

Gracchus records this connection as a matter of public fact: the earnings exist, they are declared in the Register, and they include work for a jurisdiction that was the subject of a UK-government-initiated inquiry. Gracchus does not assert any causal link to tracked Gracchus suppliers - there is none - and the record is carried as adjacent.

counterparty.name: Caribbean legal work
counterparty.note: Outside legal earnings from BVI inquiry work while MP for Torridge & West Devon. Withers LLP was the route-of-payment; the end client for the largest engagement was the Government of the British Virgin Islands. No UK supplier contract link.

timeframe.periodLabel: Dec 2019 - ongoing (outside earnings during current Parliament)

financial.personalIncomeDescription: £6m+ declared in the Register of Members' Financial Interests across his time in Parliament; >£2m in the 2019-2024 Parliament alone
financial.relatedContractsDescription: No UK government supplier contract link
